triples.head: Software updates

triples.supporting_sentence: Administrative accounts should be secured with a strong passphrase or multi-factor authentication.

triples.head: Employees

triples.head: Ransomware incidents

triples.supporting_sentence: victims are encouraged to report ransomware incidents to the ACSC at cyber.gov.au.

triples.head: Paying a ransom

triples.head: ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide

triples.supporting_sentence: The language is clear, the actions are simple, and the guidance is tailored for small and medium businesses.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide

triples.supporting_sentence: This guide is intended to complement the detailed Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) Essential Eight maturity model.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Cyber Resources for Small Businesses
triples.tail: Australian Information Security Association (AISA)
triples.supporting_sentence: ASD’s ACSC Cyber Resources for Small Businesses [is listed alongside] AISA Corporate Partnership and membership.

triples.head: SME Director
triples.tail: Oversight of cyber security risks
triples.supporting_sentence: director duties are relevant to the oversight of cyber security risks.

triples.head: SME Director
triples.tail: Being vigilant in updating computer systems, software, hardware, services, and applications
triples.supporting_sentence: An SME director should be confirming that the organisation is vigilant in updating computer systems, software, hardware, services, and applications to the latest versions.

triples.head: Unpatched vulnerabilities
triples.tail: Breaches
triples.supporting_sentence: A large percentage of breaches are due to vulnerabilities that have not been patched, or vulnerabilities that have.

triples.head: Data stocktake
triples.tail: Visibility of the key data (customer, supplier, employee)
triples.supporting_sentence: Undertake a data stocktake that provides visibility of the key data that is collected from customers, suppliers and employees.

triples.head: Unique logins (for staff)
triples.tail: Improper access/authorisation
triples.supporting_sentence: Implement access and authorisation controls, including through creating unique logins for each staff member using computer systems.

triples.head: Cyber Incident Response Plan
triples.tail: Minimising impact of cyber incident
triples.supporting_sentence: Having a documented Cyber Security Incident Response Plan is necessary for an organisation to respond swiftly, efficiently, and effectively to any incident, thereby minimising the impact.

triples.head: Business devices
triples.tail: Locked with a passphrase, PIN or biometrics
triples.supporting_sentence: Use security controls to further protect your business devices. At a minimum, they should be locked with a passphrase, PIN or biometrics.

triples.head: Devices
triples.tail: Automatically lock after a short time of inactivity
triples.supporting_sentence: Configure devices to automatically lock after a short time of inactivity.

triples.head: Shared accounts
triples.tail: Limited use and securing
triples.supporting_sentence: Limit the use of shared accounts and secure any that are used in your business.

triples.head: User access
triples.tail: Only what they need for their role
triples.supporting_sentence: Ensure each user can access only what they need for their role.

triples.head: Business devices (before disposal)
triples.tail: Perform a factory reset
triples.supporting_sentence: Perform a factory reset before selling or disposing of business devices.

triples.head: SMBs
triples.tail: Less likely to implement cyber security measures
triples.supporting_sentence: Small businesses can be particularly vulnerable, as they are less likely to implement cyber security measures that could help prevent and recover from ransomware.

triples.head: ASD Essential Eight Mitigation Strategies
triples.tail: Cost-effectiveness (for SMBs)
triples.supporting_sentence: implementing the Essential Eight Mitigation Strategies can be more cost-effective for SMBs in terms of time, money and effort than having to respond to a cyber security incident.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Outsourcing IT security

triples.supporting_sentence: Outsourcing may lead to gaps in the owner’s cyber security knowledge about which security measures are being implemented to protect their business.

triples.head: SMBs that outsource

triples.tail: Prioritising technical advice

triples.supporting_sentence: Test your backups regularly by attempting to restore data.

triples.supporting_sentence: Always keep at least one backup disconnected from your device, preferably at an offsite location in case of natural disasters or theft.

triples.tail: Malware/Ransomware (common understood risks)

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Quick Wins Guides

triples.supporting_sentence: For detailed advice on cyber supply chain risk management read our Cyber Supply Chain Guidance on cyber.gov.au.

triples.supporting_sentence: For alternative products and options, read our advice on antivirus software, by searching antivirus on cyber.gov.au.

triples.supporting_sentence: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) typically requires a combination of: something you know (password/passphrase, PIN, secret question) [and] something you have (e.g. phone or token).

triples.head: Business data
triples.tail: Understanding responsibilities to protect it

triples.head: Small businesses

triples.supporting_sentence: Read through the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) resources on website security.

triples.head: Australian Small Businesses (SMBs)
triples.tail: Lack of time, resources and expertise
triples.supporting_sentence: Australian small businesses consistently express concern over their lack of time, resources and expertise to uplift their cyber security.

triples.head: Cyber Health-Check Program
triples.tail: Lack of expertise/resources
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will create a cyber health-check program that will offer a free, tailored assessment of cyber security maturity to small and medium businesses.

triples.head: Cyber Wardens Program
triples.tail: Cyber security risks and threats
triples.supporting_sentence: The health check program will support the delivery of the existing Cyber Wardens Program for small businesses, which allows them to better understand cyber security risks and build in house capability to manage cyber threats.

triples.head: Cybercrime
triples.tail: Large-scale harm to Australian economy and national security
triples.supporting_sentence: Cybercrime, including ransomware and cyber extortion, can cause large-scale harm to the Australian economy and national security.

triples.head: Malicious state actors and cybercriminals
triples.tail: Critical services like telecommunication networks and health systems
triples.supporting_sentence: Such attacks often require minimal technical expertise and few resources, yet can have a major impact – potentially crippling core business functions and critical services like our telecommunication networks, health system, food supply chains and educational institutions.

triples.head: Australian Federal Police (AFP)
triples.tail: Investigating and disrupting criminal syndicates (Operation Aquila)
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will expand the AFP’s contribution to Operation Aquila, the AFP- and ASD-led joint standing operation aimed at investigating and disrupting criminal syndicates.

triples.head: Ransomware playbook
triples.tail: Unclear guidance on how to respond to ransom demands
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will build a ransomware playbook. This playbook will provide clear guidance to businesses and citizens on how to prepare for, deal with, and bounce back from ransom demands.

triples.head: Digital ID program
triples.tail: Need for people to share sensitive personal information
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government is continuing to develop the Digital ID program... to reduce the need for people to share sensitive personal information with government and businesses to access services online.

triples.head: Digital ID System
triples.tail: Best-practice cyber security standards (Essential Eight/ISO)
triples.supporting_sentence: the expanded program will continue to adopt best-practice cyber security standards such as ISO/IEC standards, the Australian Government’s Protective Security Policy Framework, and the Australian Cyber Security Centre’s Essential Eight.

triples.head: Products and services without built-in security
triples.tail: Vulnerabilities malicious actors can easily exploit
triples.supporting_sentence: Products and services without built-in security can present vulnerabilities that malicious actors can easily exploit, potentially undermining public trust in technology.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Developing framework for assessing national security risks of vendor products
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will develop a framework for assessing the national security risks presented by vendor products and services operating within and entering the Australian economy.

triples.head: Sensitive and critical datasets
triples.tail: Identification and protection of national significance
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will identify Australia’s most sensitive and critical datasets across the economy – particularly those that are not appropriately protected under existing regulations, yet are crucial to our national interests.

triples.head: Australian Government entities
triples.tail: Implementation of ASD’s Essential Eight strategies
triples.supporting_sentence: many government systems do not yet meet the ASD’s Essential Eight strategies for mitigating cyber security incidents. To uplift our collective cyber security, the Government must itself adopt cyber best practices.

triples.head: Critical infrastructure
triples.tail: Strengthened cyber security obligations and compliance
triples.supporting_sentence: To achieve our 2030 vision, the Australian Government will: strengthen cyber security obligations and compliance for critical infrastructure.

triples.head: Managed service providers
triples.tail: Clarified cyber security obligations
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will also seek to clarify cyber security obligations for managed service providers, aligning closely with data protection initiatives established under Shield 2.

triples.head: ASD’s Essential Eight mitigation strategies
triples.tail: Risk of compromise (government systems)
triples.supporting_sentence: This approach to the uplift of government systems will be designed to reduce the risk of compromise, and help to prevent the most common techniques used by malicious cyber actors.

triples.head: Highly sophisticated nation states and state-sponsored actors
triples.tail: Governments and critical infrastructure providers
triples.supporting_sentence: Highly sophisticated nation states and state-sponsored actors continue to target governments and critical infrastructure providers.

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.tail: Especially vulnerable to cyber security threats
triples.supporting_sentence: At the other end of the scale, SMEs are especially vulnerable to cyber security threats.

triples.head: Australian Government

triples.supporting_sentence: This Strategy includes an $8.3 million Cyber Security Connect and Protect Program, which will assist SMEs through tailored advice and assistance from trusted sources.

triples.head: Government and large businesses
triples.tail: Assisting small businesses to upgrade their cyber security
triples.supporting_sentence: Government and large businesses will assist small businesses to upgrade their cyber security and grow their cyber security awareness.

triples.head: Threat-blocking technology

triples.supporting_sentence: Supporting the roll-out of threat-blocking technology will prevent known malicious cyber threats from reaching Australian consumers and businesses.

triples.head: Voluntary Code of Practice: Securing the Internet of Things for Consumers

triples.supporting_sentence: the Australian Government will release the voluntary Code of Practice: Securing the Internet of Things for Consumers, to inform businesses about the cyber security features expected of internet-connected devices available in Australia.

triples.head: Cyber Security National Workforce Growth Program

triples.supporting_sentence: The Cyber Security National Workforce Growth Program seeks to grow cyber security skills at all stages of education, including primary, secondary and tertiary.

triples.head: Cyber security incidents

triples.head: Cyber supply chain risk management activities

triples.supporting_sentence: ASD utilises this understanding [from incident reports] to assist in the development of new and updated cybersecurity advice, capabilities, and techniques to better prevent and respond to evolving cyberthreats.

triples.head: SMBs that outsource

triples.supporting_sentence: Outsourcing may lead to gaps in the owner’s cyber security knowledge about which security measures are being implemented to protect their business.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Technical advice (ACSC)
triples.tail: Prioritisation so SMBs know which practices to implement first
triples.supporting_sentence: Technical advice must be prioritised so that Australian SMBs know which cyber security practices to implement first for best results.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Guidance for Australian SMBs
triples.tail: Effective, low cost tips that are easy to implement
triples.supporting_sentence: Guidance for Australian SMBs must be comprised of effective, low cost tips (in both time and money) that are easy to implement.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Malware, Phishing, and Ransomware
triples.tail: Most commonly understood cyber security risks (by SMBs)
triples.supporting_sentence: Data shows that Malware, Phishing and Ransomware are the most commonly understood cyber security risks.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Cyber insurers
triples.tail: Help businesses understand how to improve their cybersecurity
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber insurers... could be well-placed to help businesses understand how to improve their cybersecurity.

triples.head: SMEs
triples.tail: Underestimate cyber risk
triples.supporting_sentence: SMEs frequently believing their organization is too small to be a lucrative target, despite growing evidence to the contrary.

triples.head: Cybersecurity practices guidance
triples.tail: Littered with different guidance that can overwhelm small businesses
triples.supporting_sentence: The landscape for SMEs is therefore littered with different guidance that can overwhelm small or under-resourced security teams.

triples.head: Cyber Security Handbook
triples.tail: ASD’s Essential Eight maturity model
triples.supporting_sentence: this guide is intended to complement the detailed Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) Essential Eight maturity model.

triples.head: NDB scheme
triples.tail: Notify affected individuals and the OAIC of a material data breach
triples.supporting_sentence: The Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme – requiring an organisation to notify affected individuals and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) as soon as practicable of a material data breach.

triples.head: Australian Privacy Principle 11 (APP 11)
triples.tail: Take active measures to ensure the security of personal information
triples.supporting_sentence: Australian Privacy Principle 11... requiring an organisation to take active measures to ensure the security of personal information it holds.

triples.head: Unpatched vulnerabilities
triples.tail: A large percentage of breaches
triples.supporting_sentence: A large percentage of breaches are due to vulnerabilities that have not been patched, or vulnerabilities that have.

triples.head: Cyber Security Incident Response Plan
triples.tail: Minimising the impact of an incident
triples.supporting_sentence: Having a documented Cyber Security Incident Response Plan is necessary for an organisation to respond swiftly, efficiently, and effectively to any incident, thereby minimising the impact.

triples.head: Small business directors
triples.tail: Oversight of cyber security risks (as part of fiduciary duties)
triples.supporting_sentence: director duties are relevant to the oversight of cyber security risks, an SME director should ensure they are aware of the legal obligations.

triples.head: Ransomware incidents
triples.tail: Reporting to the ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: victims are encouraged to report ransomware incidents to the ACSC at cyber.gov.au. Sharing information about incidents helps to protect other Australian businesses.

triples.head: Paying a ransom
triples.tail: Increased likelihood of being targeted again
triples.supporting_sentence: Paying a ransom does not guarantee a victim’s files will be restored... It also increases the likelihood of a victim being targeted again.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.tail: Small and medium businesses
triples.supporting_sentence: The language is clear, the actions are simple, and the guidance is tailored for small and medium businesses.

triples.head: Ransomware reporting for businesses (Proposed)
triples.tail: Information on attack scope, timeframe, entry point, type, and data targeted
triples.supporting_sentence: Engineers Australia supports the Department’s suggested list of potential information to be reported as the main goal is to define the scope of the attack, timeframe under which it happened, entry point, type of attack, and what data was targeted.

triples.head: ‘No-fault’ and ‘no-liability’ protection principles
triples.tail: Major impact on businesses’ confidence to report ransomware incidents
triples.supporting_sentence: Engineers Australia considers it likely that ‘no-fault’ and ‘no-liability’ protection principles would have a major impact on businesses’ confidence to report ransomware or cyber extortion incidents.

triples.head: SOCI Act
triples.tail: Consideration of mandating forced deletion of personal/sensitive information
triples.supporting_sentence: The SOCI Act should consider mandating the forced deletion of personal and/or sensitive information kept for more than a reasonable amount of time.

triples.head: Reducing the amount of critical data stored
triples.tail: Attractiveness of a business as a cyber target
triples.supporting_sentence: Reducing the amount of critical data stored would be an easy way to both reduce the attractiveness of a business as a cyber target, and lower the impact should any cyber incidents happen.

triples.head: ASD and Cyber Coordinator
triples.tail: Limited use obligation on cyber incident information
triples.supporting_sentence: Engineers Australia strongly supports the limited use obligation on the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) and National Cyber Security Coordinator (Cyber Coordinator).

triples.head: Mandatory standards (Australia)
triples.tail: IoT devices supply chain protection
triples.supporting_sentence: Protecting the supply chain of IoT devices in Australia will require the participation of manufacturers, subcontractors, software developers, importers, distributors and end-users.

triples.head: Lack of knowledge (in SMEs)
triples.tail: Lack of security opening the door to cyber-attacks
triples.supporting_sentence: Lack of knowledge is too often the key issue leading to a lack of security opening the door to cyber-attacks.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Providing assistance and advice to victims of malicious cyber activity
triples.supporting_sentence: As technical cyber security leads for the Australian Government, the ACSC is uniquely positioned to provide assistance and advice to victims of malicious cyber activity, including incident response services where appropriate.

triples.head: Cyber security incident reports
triples.tail: Identifying emerging cyber campaign targeting Australia
triples.supporting_sentence: This information could help to identify an emerging cyber campaign targeting Australia and better understand the cyber threat to Australia, critical infrastructure, and specific sectors.

triples.head: Critical infrastructure asset owners and operators
triples.tail: Reporting cyber security incidents
triples.supporting_sentence: Critical infrastructure asset owners and operators may also be subject to mandatory reporting requirements from a security regulator.

triples.head: AI, ML, and LLMs
triples.tail: Increased vulnerability of Australian SMEs to cyber threats
triples.supporting_sentence: Embracing transformative technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML) and Large Language Models (LLMs) can potentially strengthen cyber security policies for Australian SMEs.

triples.head: GPT-4 model
triples.tail: Formulating cyber security policies for SMEs
triples.supporting_sentence: The GPT-4 model was identified as the most potent AI model for formulating cyber security policies for SMEs.

triples.head: Small size and limited financial resources (SMEs)
triples.tail: Susceptibility to cyber threats
triples.supporting_sentence: This susceptibility arises from a combination of factors including a shortage of cybersecurity knowledge and resources... limited financial resources, and the absence of resident IT staff.

triples.head: Increased digitalization
triples.tail: Heightens cybersecurity risks
triples.supporting_sentence: As indicated under Action 3, increased digitalization heightens cybersecurity risks; thus, it is critical that robust requirements are in place.

triples.head: Governments
triples.tail: Robust risk management to address cybersecurity risks
triples.supporting_sentence: Governments should require robust risk management to address issues such as cybersecurity risks, and they should implement robust data privacy and protection frameworks.

triples.head: Robust data privacy and protection frameworks
triples.tail: Personal information or data
triples.supporting_sentence: Overall, data privacy and protection frameworks apply to individuals, as they focus on personal information or data, regardless of whether this is generated for personal or business reasons.

triples.head: Digital IDs
triples.tail: Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI)
triples.supporting_sentence: Three sets of DPI are considered critical: digital ID, digital payments, and data exchange in the financial sector.

triples.head: Cybersecurity policy and guidelines in Australia
triples.tail: Complicated and confusing
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber security policy and guidelines in Australia are complicated and confusing; making templated policies and procedures available freely or cheaply to SME’s would assist in helping them in navigating the mine-field of cyber security information.

triples.head: Templated policies and procedures
triples.tail: Complicated and confusing cyber security policy
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber security policy and guidelines in Australia are complicated and confusing; making templated policies and procedures available freely or cheaply to SME’s would assist in helping them in navigating the mine-field of cyber security information.

triples.head: Cybercrime
triples.tail: Financial losses from fraud and ransoms
triples.supporting_sentence: There are direct and indirect costs to cybercrime victims including: Financial losses from fraud and ransoms.

triples.head: Failure to report mandatory data breaches
triples.tail: Fines of up to $1.8 Million
triples.supporting_sentence: Fines of up to $1.8 Million from failure to report mandatory data breaches to Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC).

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.tail: ASD’s Essential Eight maturity model
triples.supporting_sentence: This guide is intended to complement the detailed Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) Essential Eight maturity model.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Providing a single source of truth for small business cyber security
triples.supporting_sentence: the Government should seek to ensure it provides a single source of truth that small business can rely on.

triples.head: Revision of the 2020 Cyber Security Strategy
triples.tail: Specifically consider barriers to small businesses improving cyber security
triples.supporting_sentence: Revision of the 2020 Cyber Security Strategy should specifically consider the barriers to small businesses improving their cyber security systems and processes.

triples.head: Introducing expectations of SaaS providers
triples.tail: Disproportionate burden of cyber threats on small businesses
triples.supporting_sentence: Consideration should be given to introducing expectations of SaaS providers to build-in cyber security measures that improve software security and reduce the disproportionate burden of cyber threats on small businesses.

triples.head: Non-prescriptive mandates
triples.tail: Cybersecurity evolves with critical technologies
triples.supporting_sentence: Creating non-prescriptive mandates that nonetheless encourage organizations to analyze the probability and severity of threats in line with technological realities is important for ensuring cybersecurity evolves with critical technologies.

triples.head: 1-10-60 Rule
triples.tail: Detect malicious cyber activity within one minute
triples.supporting_sentence: This concept challenges enterprises to detect malicious cyber activity within one minute, have a human investigate that detection within 10 minutes, and remediate or isolate any compromised assets within 60 minutes.

triples.head: Proposed legislative updates (cybersecurity)
triples.tail: Focus on principles rather than prescriptive requirements
triples.supporting_sentence: we recommend and emphasize that any proposed legislative updates focus on principles rather than prescriptive requirements and include a mechanism for periodic revisions.

triples.head: Business failure to meet data protection obligations
triples.tail: Risk to the privacy of individuals
triples.supporting_sentence: the failure of a business of any size... to meet data protection obligations may pose a risk to the privacy of individuals.

triples.head: Collaboratively-designed small business health check trust mark
triples.tail: Increasing consumer confidence
triples.supporting_sentence: The adoption of a collaboratively-designed small business health check trust mark could be of benefit, as small businesses may use it from a marketing perspective, increasing consumer confidence in their offerings.

triples.head: Cyber crime incidents
triples.tail: Reporting through the ReportCyber website
triples.supporting_sentence: Reporting cyber crime incidents through the ReportCyber website is an important way for the community to help us know the full extent to which cyber criminals are harming Australians.

triples.head: ASD’s Essential Eight
triples.tail: Australian Government agencies
triples.supporting_sentence: Australian Government agencies... will strengthen their cyber security and implement the ACSC’s Essential Eight mitigation strategies.

triples.head: Cyber Supply Chain Guidance (ACSC)
triples.tail: Cyber supply chain risk management
triples.supporting_sentence: For detailed advice on cyber supply chain risk management read our Cyber Supply Chain Guidance on cyber.gov.au.

triples.head: ACSC Cyber Alert Service
triples.tail: ASD’s ACSC Protecting Your Business and Employees
triples.supporting_sentence: ASD’s ACSC Cyber Resources for Small Businesses, ASD’s ACSC Small Business Security Guide, ASD’s ACSC Protecting Your Business and Employees, [and] ASD’s ACSC Cyber Alert Service [are listed as resources].

triples.head: ASD
triples.tail: ASD Essential Eight (baseline recommendation)
triples.supporting_sentence: The Essential Eight is the Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) prioritised list of mitigation strategies to assist businesses in protecting their systems against a range of adversaries.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: ASD Essential Eight
triples.tail: Uplift of government systems
triples.supporting_sentence: This approach to the uplift of government systems will be designed to reduce the risk of compromise...

triples.head: ASD’s Essential Eight
triples.tail: Protective Security Policy Framework (PSPF)
triples.supporting_sentence: We will implement defined controls across our networks that will be consolidated into the Australian Government Information Security Manual, and enabled through the Protective Security Policy Framework.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Establishing a new process for conducting lessons-learned reviews of significant cyber incidents
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will establish a new process for conducting lessons-learned reviews of significant cyber incidents.

triples.head: Cyber Incident Review Board (CIRB)
triples.tail: Issuing advice to government and industry on how to avoid recurrence of incidents
triples.supporting_sentence: The purpose of a CIRB should be to understand the process by which the incident happened and issue advice to government and industry on how to avoid recurrence.

triples.head: Government and large businesses
triples.tail: Providing SMEs with cyber security information and tools as part of ‘bundles’ of secure services
triples.supporting_sentence: The Australian Government will work with large businesses and service providers to provide SMEs with cyber security information and tools as part of ‘bundles’ of secure services (such as threat blocking, antivirus, and cyber security awareness training).

triples.head: Cyber extortion
triples.tail: Ransomware playbook (guidance on preparation and response)
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will build a ransomware playbook... to prepare for, deal with, and bounce back from ransom demands.

triples.head: Australian Government’s Digital ID System
triples.tail: Identity theft and fraud
triples.supporting_sentence: This will mean fewer records of individuals’ ID data and documents held by commercial and government organisations – reducing the risks and impact of identity theft and fraud.

triples.head: Ransomware business model
triples.tail: Disruption by Australian Government
triples.supporting_sentence: To make Australia a hard target for ransomware, we must disrupt the ransomware business model.

triples.head: Health sector
triples.tail: Lowest cyber maturities across industry
triples.supporting_sentence: the health sector also has one of the lowest cyber maturities across industry.

triples.head: Threat Sharing Acceleration Fund
triples.tail: Building capability of Australia’s health system to identify threats
triples.supporting_sentence: This pilot will focus on building the capability of Australia’s health system to identify threats and share intelligence between medical service providers.

triples.head: Foreign owned suppliers operating in Australia
triples.tail: Subject to a foreign government’s lawful access to data
triples.supporting_sentence: Foreign owned suppliers operating in Australia may be subject to a foreign government’s lawful access to data belonging to their customers.

triples.head: Critical online services
triples.tail: Segregation from other online services more likely to be targeted by DoS attacks
triples.supporting_sentence: Critical online services are segregated from other online services that are more likely to be targeted as part of denial-of-service attacks.

triples.head: Systems of National Significance
triples.tail: Enhanced Cyber Security Obligations
triples.supporting_sentence: Systems of National Significance... Framework elements: Enhanced Cyber Security Obligations; Positive Security Obligation; Government Assistance.

triples.head: ASD’s Essential Eight
triples.tail: Australian Government Information Security Manual
triples.supporting_sentence: To protect the Australian Government’s data and digital estate, we will build on the best practice principles established within ASD’s Essential Eight. We will also implement defined controls across our networks that will be consolidated into the Australian Government Information Security Manual.

triples.head: Transfer costs (CGT and stamp duty)
triples.tail: Inhibitor to restructuring an established business
triples.supporting_sentence: advisors considered transfer costs (CGT and stamp duty) as a frequently mentioned inhibitor to restructuring an established business.

triples.head: Client education
triples.tail: Burden of complexity and compliance costs (for restructuring)
triples.supporting_sentence: Client education, seeking advice, and reducing the number of entities adopted by a single business were some of the techniques used by advisors to lessen the burden of complexity and compliance costs.

triples.head: ASD
triples.tail: Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC)
triples.supporting_sentence: Engineers Australia strongly supports the limited use obligation on the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) and National Cyber Security Coordinator (Cyber Coordinator)... It will build on existing resources available through the... ASD’s Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC).

triples.head: Cyber crime
triples.tail: Operational disruption
triples.supporting_sentence: Cybercrime can have significant impacts... including: Operational disruption.

triples.head: SMEs
triples.tail: Cyber-attacks (increasingly)
triples.supporting_sentence: Small to Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and their supply chains are increasingly becoming the target for cyber-attacks in Australia.

triples.head: Australian SMBs
triples.tail: Recognise cyber security is important
triples.supporting_sentence: Australian SMBs know cyber security is important regardless of how they rate their level of cyber security understanding.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Cyber risk
triples.tail: Operational risk
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber risk, despite its prominence and velocity, is still an operational risk that should fit within an SME’s existing approach to risk management.

triples.head: ASD’s Essential Eight
triples.supporting_sentence: Prominent examples of such frameworks include the International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO) 27001... the NIST CSF... and the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) Essential Eight.

triples.head: Cybersecurity frameworks (NIST CSF, Essential Eight, ISO 27001)
triples.tail: SMBs not knowing where to start in the implementation of cybersecurity
triples.supporting_sentence: This lack of familiarity with frameworks possibly explains why one of the top challenges to implementing good cybersecurity is “SMBs not knowing where to start in the implementation of cybersecurity”.

triples.head: Cybersecurity legislation (NDB, APPs)
triples.tail: Targeted educational initiatives (for SMBs)
triples.supporting_sentence: Implement targeted educational initiatives to increase SMBs’ understanding of cybersecurity legislation... with particular emphasis on legislation such as the NDB legislation and the Australian Privacy Principles.

triples.head: SMBs
triples.tail: Lack of familiarity with relevant regulations and frameworks
triples.supporting_sentence: SMBs also lacked familiarity with relevant regulations and frameworks.

triples.head: New funding for Operation Aquila
triples.tail: Enhance the AFP’s offensive cyber capability to investigate and disrupt cybercrime activity
triples.supporting_sentence: New funding for Operation Aquila will enhance the AFP’s offensive cyber capability to investigate and disrupt cybercrime activity.

triples.head: ASD’s Essential Eight
triples.tail: Zero trust (whole-of-government culture)
triples.supporting_sentence: To protect the Australian Government’s data and digital estate, we will build on the best practice principles established within ASD’s Essential Eight... aiming to develop a whole-of-government zero trust culture.

triples.head: Cyber Incident Management Arrangements
triples.tail: Formally recognising businesses in government's incident response playbook
triples.supporting_sentence: The Australian Government will work with state and territory governments on formally recognising businesses in governments’ incident response playbook, known as the Cyber Incident Management Arrangements.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Invest $62.3 million in a classified national situational awareness capability
triples.supporting_sentence: Through this Strategy, the Australian Government will invest $62.3 million in a classified national situational awareness capability to better enable government to understand and respond to cyber threats to critical infrastructure and other high priority networks.

triples.head: National Cyber Security Coordinator (Cyber Coordinator)
triples.tail: Coordinating whole-of-government incident response efforts
triples.supporting_sentence: The National Cyber Security Coordinator (Cyber Coordinator) and the National Office of Cyber Security will coordinate whole-of-government incident response efforts.

triples.head: Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (SOCI Act)
triples.tail: Critical infrastructure regulation
triples.supporting_sentence: From regulation of critical infrastructure under the Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (the SOCI Act) to the protection of people, information and assets in the Australian Government under the Protective Security Policy Framework...

triples.head: ASD’s limited use obligation
triples.tail: Voluntarily provided information being used for regulatory purposes
triples.supporting_sentence: Note, under ASD’s limited use obligation, information voluntarily provided to ASD about cybersecurity incidents, or potential cybersecurity incidents, cannot be used for regulatory purposes.

triples.head: Cybercrime
triples.tail: Costing the economy up to $29 billion per year
triples.supporting_sentence: cyber incidents targeting small, medium and large Australian businesses can cost the economy up to $29 billion per year, or 1.9% of Australia’s gross domestic product (GDP).

triples.head: IT asset inventory
triples.tail: Risk of unauthorised access and data breaches
triples.supporting_sentence: The absence of an IT asset inventory hinders efficient management and protection of resources, increasing the risk of unauthorised access and data breaches.

triples.head: ASD
triples.tail: ASD Top 4 (guidelines)
triples.supporting_sentence: With the Defence Industry Security Program (DISP), Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC), the Essential 8 and Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) Top 4 being just a portion of the governing bodies and information surrounding cyber security, SME’s reported confusion...

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Supporting safe and responsible use of AI
triples.supporting_sentence: The Australian Government will support safe and responsible use of AI.

triples.head: Centralisation of cyber security systems across government
triples.tail: Opportunities for malicious actors to target smaller agencies
triples.supporting_sentence: This centralisation seeks to reduce opportunities for malicious actors to target smaller agencies with less secure IT, and will increase opportunities to focus the Australian Government’s cyber security investment.

triples.head: Cybersecurity awareness campaigns
triples.tail: Partnerships with the private sector and civil society (to extend reach)
triples.supporting_sentence: Partnerships with the private sector and civil society will be critical to extend the reach of this national campaign and maintain consistent messaging.

triples.head: Cyber security is a public good
triples.tail: Enforcement by those most capable of managing it
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber security is a public good. By 2030, all Australians should feel protected by a secure and resilient digital economy. They should feel confident that cyber security will be enforced by those most capable of managing it across each layer of the technology supply chain.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Investing $1.35 billion Cyber Enhanced Situational Awareness and Response (CESAR) package
triples.supporting_sentence: On 30 June 2020, the Australian Government announced the $1.35 billion Cyber Enhanced Situational Awareness and Response (CESAR) package.

triples.head: Critical infrastructure regulation
triples.tail: Inclusion of cyber security regulation into ‘all hazards’ requirements for aviation and maritime sectors
triples.supporting_sentence: The Government will explore options to incorporate cyber security regulation into ‘all hazards’ requirements for the aviation and maritime sectors.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.tail: Cyber security advice (for SMEs)
triples.supporting_sentence: The ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide provides tailored advice to protect against the most common cyber security incidents.

triples.head: Australian Government
triples.tail: Co-designing supply chain principles for decision makers and suppliers
triples.supporting_sentence: the Australian Government will co-design supply chain principles for decision makers and suppliers, to encourage security-by-design; transparency; and autonomy and integrity in investment, procurement and security.

triples.head: Cybersecurity accreditation frameworks
triples.tail: Professional competency requirements
triples.supporting_sentence: In the medium to longer term, this could involve exploring whether there is a need for cyber security accreditation frameworks... accompanied by professional competency requirements for maintaining accreditation.

triples.head: Cyber supply chain risk management
triples.tail: Conducted during the earliest possible stage of procurement
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber supply chain risk management activities should be conducted during the earliest possible stage of procurement of operating systems, applications, information technology (IT) equipment...

triples.head: Lack of awareness (of NDB legislation)

triples.supporting_sentence: After completing this checklist, we recommend small businesses implement Maturity Level One of the Essential Eight.

triples.head: Transfer costs (CGT and stamp duty)

triples.supporting_sentence: The results indicate that advisors considered transfer costs (CGT and stamp duty) as a frequently mentioned inhibitor to restructuring an established business; other factors were administrative costs and complexity of adopting multiple entities for the one business.

triples.head: Australian Small Businesses (SMBs)
